claim: Context engineering: systematically manage what goes into the agent's context window by identifying, collecting, and serving the right information at the right time.
actionable: Plan your agent context strategy: define clear results, map required context (instructions, tools, knowledge), and create pipelines to deliver that context.

claim: Context types in agent systems: instructions (prompts/system messages), knowledge (RAG), tools (APIs/MCP), conversation history, user preferences.
actionable: Categorize your context by type; implement separate management strategies for static (instructions) vs. dynamic (conversation history, user preferences).

claim: Agent scratchpad: keep a separate file or object for agent notes during a session, keeping information out of the main context window until needed.
actionable: Implement a scratchpad (markdown file or runtime object) for your agents to log task progress and user preferences; recall when needed rather than keeping in context.

claim: Chat history compression: summarize or prune conversation history to manage context growth; keep relevant information while removing older messages.
actionable: Set a message threshold (e.g., 15 messages) at which to trigger summarization; maintain summaries alongside new messages to preserve context.

claim: Context poisoning: false information (hallucinated by the LLM) enters context and perpetuates across future calls; validate external data before adding to context.
actionable: When agents retrieve or generate information (e.g., booking a hotel), validate it against real-time APIs before adding to the agent's context or persistent memory.

claim: Context confusion: too many overlapping tool descriptions cause agents to pick wrong tools; use RAG-based tool loading to filter by relevance per task.
actionable: Use a vector database to store tool descriptions; retrieve only the top-k most relevant tools for each user query instead of listing all tools.
